turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Number oh oh nine five two, the Wisconsin Department of Health and Human Services versus Irene Blumer. Ms. Flanagan. [Inaudible]

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. In nineteen eighty-eight, Congress enacted the spousal impoverishment protections of the Federal Medicaid Act, forty two US Code section thirteen ninety-six R five, to accomplish two competing purposes. First, Congress sought to protect spouses living at home from impoverishment when the other spouse is institutionalized and requires long-term nursing home care. Secondly, Congress sought to ensure that married couples seeking Medicaid bear a fair share of the cost of such care. This case concerns whether States have the discretion to achieve those competing goals by taking into account at the time Medicaid eligibility is determined available income which the nursing home spouse is permitted to use after eligibility to support the at-home spouse. When the nursing home spouse applies for Medicaid, section thirteen ninety-six R five permits the community spouse to retain certain income and resources to mit- to meet his own monthly maintenance needs. The statute permits an increase in the standard resource allowance, however, if the at-home spouse can show at a fair hearing that the allowance will be inadequate to provide him with income at the State-protected level once the nursing home spouse qualifies for Medicaid. When making this determination, Wisconsin, like more than thirty other States, first considers whether income available to the at-home spouse from the nursing home spouse will be sufficient to ensure the protected level of income once Medicaid eligibility occurs. This method of determining whether to increase or to substitute the standard resource allowance is called the income-first rule. [Inaudible]

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: And what do the other States do?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: The the remaining States use a methodology called resource first, in which they look first to the additional resources above the standard resource allowance. The- These cases only arise where the couple has resources above the standard allowance. In...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: this case...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It'd be very helpful to me if, right at this point, you pointed the statutory pointed out the statutory provision that authorizes the State to transfer income at this stage.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: The the statutory provision, I think, specifically is found in forty-two US Code thirteen ninety-six A, sub A seven- sub seventeen, which deals with State standards for eligibility and and the Secretary's authority to set standards for...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: determining availability.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: a handy reference in the brief somewhere to the, where we can see that?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: It's in the Attor- the Solicitor General's appendix at the first thing in their appendix is the codified statute, thirty- s- thirteen ninety-six R five, the one we are discussing primarily, and no, I'm sorry, thirteen ninety-six A is in the first thing in the Solicitor General's appendix.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Page one A?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Yes, and A seventeen.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: That's eight A.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Eight A?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It looks like seven...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Honor, it is.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Then where in s- s- number seventeen is the language that you're answering Justice Stevens with?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Okay, I- A A seventeen provides that the Secretary shall include reasonable standards, and then you skip the one pareth- parenthetical, for determining eligibility for and the extent of medical assistance under the State plan, and then under B, provide for taking into account only such income and resources as are as determined in accordance with standards prescribed by the Secretary available to the applicant or recipient, et cetera.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well,

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: And...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: let me ask you a question on this point, if I may.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And you're talking now about post eligibility?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: We're talking about a determination that's made at the point of eligibility, but which concerns income available post eligibility.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, it says pre-eligibility none of the income of the community spouse shall be deemed available...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to the institutionalized spouse.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And that provision wouldn't make sense if income of the community spouse itself included income of the institutionalized spouse.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Well, I think, Your Honor, the...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Would it? That wouldn't make any sense.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: I think you have to take into account that you're talking about income being calculated at different points in the temporal spectrum for different purposes.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, you're you seem to be arguing that the phrase, community spouse's income in C in E two C includes income from the institutionalized spouse,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: it can't under that section I read, I think.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: how you got there. Uh-huh.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: refers only to prohibiting income of the community spouse from being deemed available...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to the nursing home.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: point is undoubtedly correct that income of the community spouse there means income of the community spouse alone, not any attri- attributed income from the institutionalized spouse, right?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Isn't that right? It It has to mean only the income of the community spouse.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think you have to look at...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: what's available at that point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's that's...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Do you Do you have any other section in the act in which the phrase, income of the community spouse, means not just the income of the s- p- community spouse alone, but also income that has been attributed from the institutionalized spouse?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Under the definition of community spouse, income maintenance allowance, I believe that which is under subsection D to B...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: D to what?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: D to B, refers...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Can you tell us where in the app- in the SG's appendix that is?

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It's on fifty-nine A of your cert petition.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Eighteen A of the...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Fifty-nine A.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: At any rate, that that particular section refers to monthly income otherwise available to the community spouse, and the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: our position...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: is that this evidences an recognition of the of the fact, as Medicaid has long recognized, that spouses are required to s- support one another, and that this is a a background rule.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Ms. Flanagan, it might help if I think one of the main features of this legislation was that income from the community spouse was never to be attributed to the institutionalized spouse, but vice versa, there there is no such prohibition.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: That's right,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: wha- -- none of...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: this makes sense unless you appreciate that there that was an absolute prohibition.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: institutionalized...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: subsection B one, thirteen ninety-six R five B one, and that was referred to...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: That was the section I read to you...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: in my question.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Could could I have an answer to my question? The the section you just referred me to is still not another section other than the one at issue here in which the simple phrase, income of the community spouse, is used in a sense that means the the community spouse's own income plus any income attributed to the community spouse from the institutionalized spouse.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: I don't believe it's used anywhere else.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: means.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: no, I think that rather solves the difficulty, frankly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I disagree...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: think that ins- that income of the of the community spouse means income of the community spouse, and you say it means no, the community spouse's own income plus attributed income.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: income.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: respect, Your Honor, the Medicaid statute has long considered available income as part of the income of the person to which it's referring, and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: we...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: does that, that's all I'm asking for.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Th- that particular phrase is not used frequently in this statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: used specifically  here.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: the the situation you're talking about we're deeming in the other direction, where they deem the community spouse income to be attributable to the institutionalized spouse, not vice versa?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, Justice Stevens, the the background rule which I referred to which this Court clearly articulated in Gray Panthers case is that spouses are expected to support one another.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: But that was for purposes of determining how much of the community spouse's income should be deemed to belong to the institutionalized spouse.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: street,

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: and the Court clearly recognized that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: soon as eligibility occurs.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: that the resource-first rule gives greater protection to the community spouse than the income-first rule?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: The the result is that it permits the in general it frequently permits the at-home spouse to retain a greater share of the couple's joint resources than would be the case under the State-defined standard resource allowance, and in that sense, yes, that's definitely correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Ms. Flanagan, am am I right in thinking that the, neither the act we're talking about nor the SSI actually define community spouse's income?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: No, it doesn't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: considered.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: income-first rule in Wisconsin adopted by the legislature, or by a State agency? Or...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: It was initially adopted as a matter of policy by the State agency immediately after passage of the statute.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So then your State court, I take it, under prevailing Wisconsin rules, could not ignore the legislature's determination unless it found that the Federal statute was unambiguous.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: That well, that is what they did, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Ms. Flanagan, as I understand it, the there's a provision and these have been referred to this morning, but there's a provision that forbids attribution from the community spouse to the institutionalized spouse period, no qualifications on that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: During institutionalization.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: There's also a provision which recognizes the possibility of transferring income from the institutionalized spouse to the community spouse after eligibility has been determined, but does not require any such transfer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: basically correct?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: require the transfer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: the statute...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: to...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: R-

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: basically require them to do it.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: But it doesn't, that latter provision doesn't make any reference to the period before eligibility, and I guess my question is, why don't we why don't we infer some kind of a negative inference when when the provision refers totally to the post eligibility period, why don't we find some negative implication that it was not expected in the pre-eligibility period?

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Well, the fact is that the calculation that ta- the the hearing officers ask to be made here concerns the post eligibility period.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: So so basically the answer is, the fair hearing has got to take place before eligibility is determined , and that's in effect the answer to my...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: question.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: but the calculation is looking ahead,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: if there are no...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And if you can't give me an answer right away, maybe you can when you come back.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Well, that really doesn't arise because of subsection B one, which expressly precludes that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Ms. Flanagan.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mr. Lamken, you've heard the questions, and it is difficult, looking at the text of the statute, to figure out what supports the petitioner's view, although, as I understand it, that is also the view of the Federal Government here.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: our...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: are there proposed regulations of HHS that would allow either resource-first or income-first rules?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: How far along is that process? When is that going to be adopted?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: November sixth.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Can I ask what authority the Secretary has to say that the statute is ambiguous, so it can mean either one? We don't even let Federal agencies do that under Chevron.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Justice Scalia, I think the answer comes in two parts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: In addition, this...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: that about e- every ambiguity, I mean, that there are two different methodologies.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: point source,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: yeah im- yeah point source of emission.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: In fact, Justice Scalia, this Court has upheld precisely that type of regulation issued by the Secretary.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and are reasonable, they must be withheld -- upheld.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: if I understand it correctly, there is express statutory authorization for the refour- resource-first method, whereas the income-first method is drawn by inference from what you consider ambiguities?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Would you go over that a little more slowly...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for me? How do you read B one to cover reverse...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: deeming as well as deeming?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: The inference to be drawn from that is that there is no prohibition on deeming income of the institutionalized spouse...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: But even if there's no prohibition, where is your authorization for doing this? That's what I don't find in the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: statute.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Your Honor, it was a it's a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: se-

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: you start from a background rule of the name on the check as a background rule for the whole SSI program, how can you change that rule without authorization?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: That's the mistake, Justice...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: That's the mistake, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to support...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: statute does it say that?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: The statute doesn't but st- Congress said it when it enacted the Medicaid Act in the first instance, and that was the established rule under the Secretary's policies at the time that this statute was enacted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: the income of one spouse may be deemed to another.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: That was deeming, not reverse deeming.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, Your Honor. In fact,

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: deeming did occur reverse deeming did occur, or could occur under the prior policies, particularly in section two oh nine B states.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: spouse...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: leave that background principle in place, and we can all concede that it's in place, without thereby coming to the belief that when you say income of the community spouse, you mean, income of the community spouse plus whatever is deemed attributable to the community spouse.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Your Honor, it would be the the Secretary's or the regulations that existed at this time, when they discussed what we count as your income, as your applicant for SSI, for example, it said, we count as your income your income plus income from other people, so that it treated it as the individual's income, and that is consistent with the background principle that sp- each spouse's income is income to the other spouse, and when States may establish reasonable standards...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: sorry what did it mean, income from other people?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Their...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: account...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: given you by your children on a regular basis, and things of that sort?

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: Well, actually attributed inc- income, Justice Scalia, actual income that's passed over you don't need a deeming rule, because...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that's actually...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you you you...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: don't...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: mean that.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: for responsible individuals there were categories, such as spouses, such as parents, such as there is another category I can't remember the name of, but where somebody had e- e- responsibility for supporting you, their income was deemed to be your income for determining your eligibility.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And that...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: it the only thing that you've got expressed in the record anywhere to indicate that that really is what Congress had in mind is the statement in the legislative history that is quoted in the briefs that refers to other income attributed,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: is that right? So y-

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: That's the only thing in black and white.

turns.name: JEFFREY A. LAMKEN
turns.text: That is the only thing in black and white, other than the fact that the settled background principles the Secretary operated under before the enactment would treat the income of one spouse as available to the other.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: but deeming in the other direction occurred.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Lamken.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Counsel, would you mind telling us why it matters which rule is followed by a State, resource for first or income first, not just in an individual case, but overall? Who who saves what in terms of money if you do one thing or the other?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Yes, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Doesn't it also make possible...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: the the payments, the the actual payments start earlier? I mean, the the reason this this was of such concern is that the institutionalized spouse would not be eligible monthly for checks, so that the immediate effect was to she could pay down more rapidly what was her excess resources before she qualified.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The the Yes, so that bu- Justice O'Connor asked you what the effect of when I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: the most immediate effect of, is she starts to collect Medicaid sooner and doesn't use...

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: the...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: spousal resources.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Now I understand your question.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Maybe I'm not making myself clear.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, that's...

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: correct.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: But but doesn't that assume your case and not the more typical case, the more typical case, e- given statistical projections, is that the husband will be the institutionalized per- and so in the typical case it will not work to the advantage of the couple?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well I I s- I would agree with you, Justice Kennedy, that the typical case is statistically that it is the husband that goes into the nursing home first, and we don't have that case here today, but I I believe I disagree with you a- as to the effect that this has.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: I don't think I answered your question, Justice Kennedy i- But the i- the institutionalized spouse, if it is the high- er Are Am I are you asking me whether, if the institutionalized spouse has a higher income, that that what happened here won't happen? Is that the question,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: or that...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: yi- Yes.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: That's correct, so in fact in many cases the income first rule will have a worse effect when the husband is the one that goes in first.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well if if...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: States cannot follow this income-first rule, maybe they would just respond by reducing the minimum monthly maintenance needs allowance and adjust it that way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Or adjust downward the resources protectable for the community spouse.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: How many States are using income first?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: We don't exactly know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Of course,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I suppose a really hard-nosed State could do both, right, could use the income-first rule plus plus adjust downward the the other I mean, th- the the two don't go with each other.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: downward or use the income first.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: if they do it this way.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What is your burden if it is ambiguous? If it is ambiguous, do do you lose, do you acknowledge that you lose?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Oh, absolutely not Justice Scalia.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Do you think the ambiguity has -- Do you think the ambiguity has to be resolved, or can can the Secretary just just leave the ambiguity floating out there?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, that's essentially what they've decided to do...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: in the proposed...

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: rule is to leave it floating.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to do it.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: By proper, you mean lawful? y-

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Lawful, that's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: are not permitted to do that?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, what...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: i- i- with...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: a case like Batterton against Francis, then?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I believe that in a case like Batterton B V Francis, w- we have a different set of rules here.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, but you know, it's still the general same ball park.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well, you say the question is resolved.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: And how how does that follow?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, there's a a couple of first of all, the the authority that the Secretary has relied on to issue its proposed rule and apparently from which its authority to develop the rule at all is thirteen ninety-six A A seventeen sub B.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: one.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: This is very difficult to take...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in aurally.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: But go ahead if you want.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: It's almost as difficult to say it.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: May I ask you a question? I know a case is easier if you don't look at the legislative history, and so it's probably easier for my colleague than it is for me.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, i- i- I've two responses to that, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: In In that particular provision, to retain an adequate amount of resources, all that all that any other income attributable to the community spouse need mean is income attributable to him from sources other than interest on on on his on his resources.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: The institutionalized spouse's wages, or the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The community spouse's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Oh, yes,

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: absolutely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I think it does.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I mean it...

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Yes, I believe that it does...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: is the resources that provide income, which means, you know, stocks or whatever, and all that phrase there need mean is something, any any other income attributable to him from something other than his stocks.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Such as his wages,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: right?

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: You your argument in in the event that we find ambiguity is I guess boils down simply to the fact that for a variety of reasons it would frustrate the congressional policy behind the act itself if we held against you, and yet in a in a way, haven't you provided a an answer to that, a counter to that argument in in your answer to a question a few moments ago? You you said, and I I think have to say, that if if the States lose on the particular issue before us here, the States as a practical matter can get to the same kind of rough dollar and cents result simply by adjusting the the the the the amount of resources, that is the the the the th- the baseline amount for the community spouse to retain and the amount of income which is thought to be necessary for the community spouse to to live decently, so it it it almost seems as though it doesn't very much matter, necessarily, to the enactment of whatever policy Congress had, whether the flexibility comes in income first versus resource first, or whether it comes in setting the the the allowances for income and assets.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, I think that the answer to that is that the resource-first allowance, refor- resource-first rule was placed in a provision that is what we call the fail safe provision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: but in...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: this is kind of an exceptional case kind of mechanism, regardless of how you set income and resources.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: And as an as a as an exceptional case mechanism, it's only going to work if it works the way you say, on a resource-first basis?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: That's right, and and to stress the exceptional case component of it, you have to remember how you get one of these hearings.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Have you read the notice of the proposed rulemaking?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Yes, I have, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And I suppose your argument is to the effect that that is just not a permissible interpretation of the statute?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Yes, that's certainly one of our arguments against it.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Do we owe any deference to the agency here in its interpretation?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, because our position is that the position they're taking is totally unreasonable, no, you don't owe any deference to the agency.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Does the income-first rule mean that at the end of the day less Federal money is spent on Medicaid care?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Not necessarily.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Would you comment on on c one of the questions I asked Mr. Lamken, whether the background rule is the name-on-the-check rule, or as he puts it, the ri- the better view is the background rule is one of deeming, and so that we should start from the premise that it's okay to treat one spouse's income as part of the other spouse.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Well, even under this program, as I understand it, in the post eligibility determination the income can be transferred from the institutionalized spouse to the community spouse.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I might ask one question.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Tha- that was a question?

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I was putting the thing...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: because I I want you to...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: see that at the moment I'm thinking, well, I can't figure it out but I'm working with those examples and and since I can't, I say, leave it up to the Secretary, leave it up to the State, leave it up to somebody else as long as the statutue allows that I I wanted to expose that to you because I want you to have a chance to say, no, you're wrong, your example is wrong, your reasoning is wrong, everything's wrong, so, go ahead.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: I I concur with everything you just said, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Although that you make a big point of that.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: It would be.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: I would have thought that your response would be the statutory language requires the result you're urging, but you don't make that argument, apparently.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: No, no, we do make that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: argument, Justice O'Connor. Yes. The statutory language definitely does require...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: hear that in response to the question.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, it was in the opening that I didn't get a chance to make, so In your response to Justice Breyer, isn't it so, or am I counsel, am...

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: wrong in thinking that under ERISA there is a requirement to provide for the surviving spouse, it isn't the option of the insured individual?

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: Well, I'm no expert on ERISA, but I believe that there is a notice requirement and a sign-off requirement in ERISA.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But it's not the insured's election in the first place.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: it isn't the wage earner's choice...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I don't want, usually her, to be any part of it.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: That's right, but I think those decisions are made at a time when long-term care is not necessarily in the immediate offing, and maybe usually at age sixty-five or thereabouts, long before nursing home stays may be inevitable, and so the couple is making an informed choice about how best to maximize their income stream.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Hagopian, I want to come back to the snippet from legislative history that's referred to on page eighteen of the Government's brief.

turns.name: MITCHELL HAGOPIAN
turns.text: I think I frankly agree with you, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Hagopian.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Know what I mean?

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Mm.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: serve...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Why is...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: that? I mean, he just...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: said on that that that and I certainly was in the briefs, that that if you say they have to spend down the three hundred thousand, they're not going to give it to the doctors in the institution.

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: Well, neither of us have any statistics on that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: also...

turns.name: MAUREEN M. FLANAGAN
turns.text: would have had money.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Ms. Flanagan.
